planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in sharply drained, sandy or rocky soil in full sun after last frost; ponytail palm stores water in its swollen base and demands excellent drainage — clay or consistently moist soil causes fatal rot.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, sandy or rocky, low-fertility soil in full sun; despite its name, ponytail palm is neither a palm nor a tree but a succulent member of the asparagus family from semi-arid Mexico, identifiable by its swollen, barrel-like base (caudex) that stores water and its long, thin, arching, grass-like leaves that cascade from the top like a ponytail. Water deeply but infrequently — monthly in summer, even less in winter — allowing the soil to dry completely between waterings. It is virtually indestructible under hot, dry conditions and tolerates severe neglect, making it one of the best drought-tolerant accent plants for zone 9–11 landscapes. Produces offsets (pups) at the base that can be removed and rooted.

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings or container plants in well-drained, average-fertility soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; firecracker plant blooms most heavily in full sun and produces flowers nearly year-round in frost-free climates.

directions: Root stem cuttings easily in moist sand or perlite, or plant container specimens in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; firecracker plant produces arching, rush-like, nearly leafless stems covered in tubular scarlet-red flowers that are among the most effective hummingbird and butterfly attractors in Gulf Coast landscapes, blooming almost continuously from spring through fall and year-round in frost-free zones. The plant forms a graceful, weeping mound that drapes beautifully over walls, slopes, and containers. Extremely drought-tolerant once established. Cut back hard in late winter if frost-damaged to encourage fresh growth; plants recover quickly. Coral and yellow-flowered selections are also available.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Direct sow or plant divisions after last frost in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; coral vine dies back to underground tubers after frost but re-sprouts vigorously in spring in zones 8–11.

directions: Direct sow seeds or plant tuberous roots after last frost in any well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; coral vine (also called Queen's Wreath or Confederate Vine) is a fast-growing Mexican native that covers fences, arbors, and trellises with a flush of vivid rose-pink flowers from midsummer through frost, with the heaviest bloom following rainfall or irrigation after a dry period. The fleshy tubers survive winter underground in zones 8–11 and re-sprout each spring with remarkable vigor — plants can grow 20–40 feet in a single season. Extremely drought-tolerant, heat-tolerant, and adaptable to nearly any soil. The small seeds are edible and have been used as a grain food; the plant occasionally self-sows in warm climates.

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings or container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; firebush grows quickly to 5–10 feet in its first season in warm climates and can be cut back hard each spring where frost kills it to the ground.

directions: Plant stem cuttings or container plants in well-drained, average-fertility soil in full sun to partial shade; firebush is a Florida and Gulf Coast native that produces clusters of tubular orange-red flowers nearly year-round in frost-free climates, followed by small dark-purple berries that songbirds, hummingbirds, and butterflies visit eagerly. The whorled leaves turn brilliant orange-red in fall and full sun. It grows fast and tolerates drought, salt spray, and coastal conditions once established. In zones 8–9 it freezes to the ground but re-sprouts vigorously from the roots in spring; cut back to the ground in late winter to promote fresh growth. 'Dwarf' selections reaching 3–4 feet are available and useful for smaller spaces.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container specimens in fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun after last frost; in zones below 10, grow in containers and bring indoors before first frost.

directions: Root cuttings in moist sand/perlite mix in summer heat or plant container specimens in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Arabian jasmine is the national flower of the Philippines and the primary source of the jasmine scent used in perfumes and jasmine tea — the double-flowered form 'Grand Duke of Tuscany' is especially fragrant, with blooms that intensify their perfume at night. Plants bloom intermittently throughout the warm season and can be trained as vines on a trellis or grown as shrubs. Fertilize monthly during active growth with a balanced fertilizer. In zone 9 grow against a warm south-facing wall and protect from hard freezes; in zone 8 and colder grow in containers. Harvest flowers in the morning for the strongest fragrance.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after soil warms to 70°F; in zones 9–11, direct sow outdoors after last frost. Roselle is a short-day plant that flowers in fall as days shorten, so planting earlier means more leaf and stem production before flowering.

directions: Start seeds indoors or sow directly after the last frost in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; roselle is a tropical hibiscus grown primarily for its deep-red, fleshy calyces that envelop the seed capsules after the flowers fade in fall — these calyces are harvested fresh or dried to make hibiscus tea (agua de Jamaica), syrups, jams, and wines with a bright cranberry-like tartness. Plants grow rapidly to 4–8 feet tall and are ornamental throughout the season with attractive burgundy-tinged stems and lobed leaves. Harvest calyces when they are plump and fully colored but before they dry on the plant; check plants every few days as the harvest window is short. A single plant can yield 1–3 pounds of fresh calyces.

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in well-drained soil in full sun in spring; yellow trumpet tree grows quickly — 2–3 feet per year in warm climates — and can be used as a street tree, patio specimen, or large accent plant.

directions: Plant container specimens in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; yellow trumpet tree is one of the most spectacular flowering trees in the tropical and subtropical world, producing an explosion of brilliant golden-yellow trumpet-shaped flowers completely covering the bare branches in late winter to early spring before the new leaf flush — the brief but unforgettable show stops traffic when the tree is in peak bloom. Trees grow 15–30 feet tall and wide and tolerate drought, poor soils, and salt once established. Established plants are somewhat cold-hardy to 28°F for brief periods; young trees require frost protection in zone 9 until established. The wood is extraordinarily dense and durable, historically used for bow-making and tool handles.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost or direct sow after frost; in zones 9–11 cut plants back to 6 inches in late fall and early winter to interrupt the year-round bloom cycle and deter Monarch butterflies from overwintering rather than migrating.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost or sow directly after frost danger passes in well-drained, average soil in full sun; tropical milkweed produces orange-and-yellow bicolor flowers continuously from late spring through frost and is the most reliably available milkweed for Monarch butterfly gardeners in warm climates, serving as both a nectar source and larval host plant. In frost-free zones (9–11), cut plants back to 6 inches in November–January to remove OE parasite spores that accumulate on the foliage and to prevent Monarchs from staying put year-round rather than migrating — a critical management step that affects monarch population health. Plants regrow rapidly after cutting. Native milkweed species (A. incarnata, A. tuberosa) are preferable where they grow reliably.
